summary: Down syndrome is caused by trisomy of chromosome 21, and the risk increases with advanced maternal age.
Down syndrome is caused by monosomy of chromosome 21.
Down syndrome risk is primarily associated with advanced paternal age.
Turner syndrome (45,X) presents with short stature, webbed neck, and ovarian dysgenesis in phenotypic females.
Turner syndrome (47,XXX) presents with tall stature and normal ovarian function.
Turner syndrome affects only phenotypic males with hypogonadism.
Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) presents with tall stature, gynaecomastia, small testes, and infertility in males.
Klinefelter syndrome (45,X) presents with short stature and ambiguous genitalia.
Klinefelter syndrome has no effect on fertility or testosterone levels.
A Robertsonian translocation involves fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes at their centromeres, and balanced carriers have 45 chromosomes.
A Robertsonian translocation involves fusion of two metacentric chromosomes and carriers have 44 chromosomes.
Balanced Robertsonian translocation carriers always show clinical abnormalities.
Mosaicism results from mitotic nondisjunction after fertilisation, producing two or more genetically different cell lines in one individual.
Mosaicism results from errors during meiosis I in the parent.
Mosaicism always produces a uniform clinical phenotype regardless of cell line distribution.
Edwards syndrome (trisomy 18) presents with clenched fists with overlapping fingers, rocker-bottom feet, and congenital heart defects.
Edwards syndrome is trisomy 13 with cleft lip and polydactyly.
Edwards syndrome is compatible with normal lifespan in most patients.
Patau syndrome (trisomy 13) presents with holoprosencephaly, cleft lip/palate, and polydactyly.
Patau syndrome is trisomy 18 with clenched fists and rocker-bottom feet.
Patau syndrome has a better prognosis than Down syndrome.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review chromosomal aberrations and their clinical features.
Good effort! Revise the karyotypes and clinical presentations.
